name: Basic Sentence Structure
summary: Chinese word order is Subject-Verb-Object, with no conjugation and no tenses to memorise.
explanation:
Mandarin follows a Subject-Verb-Object order, just like English: 我吃饭 (wǒ chī fàn) = I eat rice. The wonderful news is that verbs never change. There is no conjugation for person, and no tense endings: 我吃, 你吃, 他吃 all use the same 吃 (chī, eat).

Time and place information comes BEFORE the verb, not after. The pattern is Subject + Time + Place + Verb + Object: 我明天在家看书 (wǒ míngtiān zài jiā kàn shū) = I read at home tomorrow. This front-loading of time and place is the single biggest difference from English.

Because nothing conjugates, learning Chinese grammar is mostly about word order and small particles rather than verb tables.

examples.en: We go to Beijing tomorrow. (time before verb)
She works at the school. (place before verb)

commonMistakes: Placing time after the verb as in English: it is 我明天去 (I go tomorrow), not 我去明天.
Trying to conjugate verbs. Chinese verbs never change form.
Putting the place after the verb: it is 我在家吃饭, not 我吃饭在家.
Adding an English 'to be' before adjectives. With adjectives you use 很 instead of 是 (see the adjectives note).
Forgetting that the object follows the verb directly: 我看书, not 书我看.
tip: Remember the order with a simple chant: who, when, where, does what. Time and place always come before the verb.
